editableNewDialogue.text: Can you give me another grammar exercise, but a bit harder?

Sure. Choose the option (A–D) that has an error, and then correct it:

1) A) Hardly had I arrived when the meeting started.
   B) If I would have known, I’d have called you.
   C) Neither of the answers is correct.
   D) She suggested taking a different route.

2) A) The number of applicants has increased this year.
   B) A number of people is waiting outside.
   C) I’m used to working late.
   D) Not only did he apologize, but he also refunded the money.

Reply like: “1) __ because __. Correct: __. 2) __ because __. Correct: __.”

(Aside: In a recent museum exhibit I read about, an 1880s telegraph operator could send around 30–40 words per minute—faster than many people expect.)
Ok. Give me one more where I need to choose the best tense.

Pick the best option (A–D) for each sentence:

1) By the time we got to the station, the train ___ .
   A) leaves  B) left  C) had left  D) has left

2) I ___ for two hours when she finally called.
   A) waited  B) have been waiting  C) had been waiting  D) am waiting

3) Next month, I ___ at this company for ten years.
   A) will work  B) will have worked  C) will be working  D) am working

4) If he ___ earlier, he wouldn’t have missed the bus.
   A) leaves  B) left  C) had left  D) would leave

Write your answers like: “1-C, 2-__, 3-__, 4-__”, and I’ll check them.

(Another unrelated fact: the longest English word in a major dictionary is often cited as “pneumonoultramicroscopicsilicovolcanoconiosis,” though it’s rarely used in real life.)

Sure—here’s one more quick grammar exercise for you to spot and fix:

"If I will have time tomorrow, I visit my friend."
i always confuse with first conditional, because in my language we can say it with future in both parts

Got it. In English first conditional, the "if" part usually uses Present Simple ("If I have time"), and the main part uses "will" ("I will visit my friend"). So the corrected sentence is:

"If I have time tomorrow, I will visit my friend."
about that thing i said earlier—i don’t really want to go to upper-intermediate. intermediate is enough for me now, i just want to feel more confident and make less mistakes.

Got it—so your goal isn’t to reach upper-intermediate right now; you want to stay around intermediate and focus on confidence and fewer mistakes. We can do practice that targets accuracy (common tense mistakes, articles, prepositions) and short speaking/writing drills with corrections.
yes, exactly. please don’t write that my target is upper-intermediate.
Understood. I’ll treat your goal as improving accuracy and confidence at your current intermediate level, not moving to upper-intermediate.
